narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 39 and your upper-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' academic and background details, estimated a 62.6% chance that you would not pass it—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower academic rankings in school: you placed in the bottom decile for both your first-year and third-year grades, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting bar exam failure. While your LSAT score was above average and helped pull the prediction in a more favorable direction, it wasn't quite enough to offset those early academic struggles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, below the typical 3.21 average, also nudged things slightly toward the riskier side, though less forcefully than your school deciles. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of applicants and offered a small positive boost. Compared to typical students who get approved, your school deciles were notably weaker than the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5th or 6th, which often signal stronger bar exam readiness in the model's view. We know these numbers don't capture your full potential, resilience, or unique journey as a Black woman breaking barriers in law. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your academic deciles, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA improvement resources, and reapplying with updated credentials that highlight your growth. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas head-on. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing.
